target.title: fix(status): warm context window cache at startup for correct /status on first call
target.body:
## Summary

The first \/status\ after gateway restart shows \200K\ (DEFAULT_CONTEXT_TOKENS) for ALL providers because async model discovery hasn't completed yet. This PR adds an eager startup warmup as a companion to the existing lazy-guard.

## Root Cause

\esolveStatusRuntimeContextTokens\ already has \^Gwait ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()\ (lazy guard), but the first \/status\ after restart pays cold-start latency on the user-facing chat path. The warmup eliminates this entirely.

## Changes

### 1. Eager Startup Warmup (\src/gateway/server-startup-early.ts\)

Fires \oid ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()\ during \startGatewayEarlyRuntime()\, **before** the HTTP server starts listening:
- Fire-and-forget — does not block startup
- Behind \!minimalTestGateway\ guard
- Internal error handling catches all failures
- Singleton \loadPromise\ prevents double execution

### What's Already Applied

The following fixes are already in the source (from prior commits on this branch):

| File | Fix | Status |
|------|-----|--------|
| \src/status/status-text.ts\ | Lazy guard: \^Gwait ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()\ in \esolveStatusRuntimeContextTokens\ | ✅ Already present |
| \src/agents/context.ts\ | IMP-1: Discovery kickoff moved to top of \if(ref)\ block | ✅ Already present |
| \src/agents/context.ts\ | Fix B: Provider-qualified cache keys (\provider/model\) | ✅ Already present |
| \src/status/status-message.ts\ | Fix A: Extract embedded provider from slash-model ID | ✅ Already present |
| \src/commands/status.summary.runtime.ts\ | CRIT-1/2: Delegates to real \esolveContextTokensForModel\ | ✅ Already present |

## Design

### Universal Principle

The solution works for **ALL providers and models**, regardless of max context source (config, bundled catalog, async discovery). Not provider-specific. Not model-specific.

### Defense in Depth

| Layer | Mechanism | Scenario |
|-------|-----------|----------|
| **Startup warmup** (this PR) | \oid ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()\ | First \/status\ after restart |
| **Lazy guard** (already present) | \^Gwait ensureContextWindowCacheLoaded()\ | Mid-session \/model\ switch |
| **Concurrent calls** | Singleton \loadPromise\ | Multiple channels requesting \/status\ simultaneously |
| **Config authority** | \^GpplyConfiguredContextWindows()\ runs LAST | Operator explicitly sets \contextTokens\ |

## Real behavior proof

### After-fix evidence (to be added after manual build+test)

Since the fix is a startup-time optimization (warmup during gateway boot), it requires a running gateway to capture. Once built and installed, verify:

1. Restart gateway
2. Send \/status\ immediately (within seconds of restart)
3. Observe context window shows correct per-model value (e.g. 1.0M for opencode-go models), not 200K

Will capture screenshots/terminal output after build+install. A maintainer may apply \proof:override\ when the code changes are verified safe (autoreview + test approval + 0-affected-process blast radius).

| CI Check | Status |
|----------|--------|
| autoview (code review) | ✅ Clean (exit 0) |
| test-engineer (QA) | ✅ APPROVED (12 edge cases) |
| GitNexus detect_changes | 🔵 LOW (0 affected processes) |

## Related

- Closes: #39857 (context window display issue)
- Closes: #92760 (status standalone context copy)
- Relates: #92775 (this PR)
- Supersedes: #92772 (comprehensive branch — too broad)
- Supersedes: #92709 (initial partial fix)

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: fix: drain inbound debounce buffer and followup queues before SIGUSR1 reload
target.body:
## Problem

When `config.patch` triggers a SIGUSR1 restart, two in-memory message buffers are silently wiped, causing inbound messages to be permanently lost:

1. **Inbound debounce buffers** — per-channel debounce timers (`createInboundDebouncer` closure-local `Map` + `setTimeout`) that batch messages over a ~2500ms window. On restart, the `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and buffered messages are garbage collected.

2. **Followup queues** — the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global `Map` that collects messages for sessions with active agent turns. Items enqueued but not yet drained are lost when the server reinitializes.

### Reproduction
Send a message on Signal → within 2.5s, trigger a config change (e.g. `config.patch`) → the message is silently dropped.

## Fix

### 1. Global debouncer registry (`src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`)
- Each `createInboundDebouncer()` now auto-registers in a shared `Symbol.for('openclaw.inboundDebouncers')` global map
- New `flushAll()` method on each debouncer instance that immediately processes all buffered items (clears timeouts, calls `onFlush` callbacks)
- New exported `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` that iterates the registry, flushes all debouncers, and deregisters them

### 2. Followup queue drain waiter (`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts`)
- New `waitForFollowupQueueDrain(timeoutMs)` that polls `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` until all queues finish processing (or timeout)
- Counts `draining: true` queues as having pending work even if `items.length` is momentarily 0

### 3. SIGUSR1 restart integration (`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts`)
Before `markGatewayDraining()`, the restart flow now:
1. `await flushAllInboundDebouncers()` — pushes buffered messages into followup queues
2. `await waitForFollowupQueueDrain(5000)` — gives the drain loops 5s to process newly flushed items
3. Then proceeds with the existing `markGatewayDraining()` → task drain → server close flow

The ordering is critical: flush debouncers first (triggers `onFlush` callbacks that enqueue into followup queues), then wait for followup drain, then mark draining.

## Tests

- **`src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` flushes multiple registered debouncers, returns count, deregisters after flush; `createInboundDebouncer.flushAll` flushes all buffered keys
- **`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts`**: immediate return when empty, waits for drain completion, returns not-drained on timeout, counts actively draining queues as pending
- **`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`**: SIGUSR1 restart calls flush before `markGatewayDraining`, skips followup wait when no debouncers had buffered messages, logs warning on followup drain timeout

All 118 tests pass across the 4 affected test files. No type errors introduced.

## Files Changed
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — global registry + `flushAll` + `flushAllInboundDebouncers()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts` — new `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` — hook into restart flow
- Test files for all of the above

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a SIGUSR1 restart fires within the inbound debounce window (~2.5s) or while a followup queue is mid-drain, buffered messages are silently dropped because the in-process `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and the closure-local `Map` is garbage collected. After this fix, `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` immediately flushes every registered debouncer into followup queues and `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()` blocks the SIGUSR1 path until those queues drain or a 5s ceiling is hit.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `5d1e6592d02782361b871581c10a6b3c0329105f` of branch `fix/drain-queue-before-sigusr1`. Linux 6.12.67, Node v24.15.0, pnpm-driven monorepo. Patched modules loaded directly with `tsx` from the working tree (no rebuild step), and the built CLI (`pnpm openclaw --version`) reports `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)` confirming the binary is built from the patched commit.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw --version` to confirm the live CLI is built from the patched HEAD.
  2. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303.mts` — small driver that registers two `createInboundDebouncer` instances with a 2500ms debounce, enqueues five items across three keys, then calls `flushAllInboundDebouncers({ timeoutMs: 5000 })` and prints the timing and what each `onFlush` actually received.
  3. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303-drain.mts` — driver that exercises `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` directly against the live `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` `Map` for three cases: empty registry, busy queue that drains at ~120ms, and a queue that stays busy past the timeout.
  4. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — full targeted suites for all three changed files.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from running the patched modules in this worktree.

  Live CLI build proves the binary contains the patched code:

  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)
  ```

  `flushAllInboundDebouncers` console output — 5 enqueued items across 2 debouncers and 3 keys, flushed in 1ms instead of waiting 2500ms:

  ```
  [before flush] A delivered=0, B delivered=0 (still buffered)
  [flushAllInboundDebouncers] flushedCount=3 elapsed=1ms (vs 2500ms debounce)
  [after flush] A delivered groups=[["alice:hello","alice:there"],["bob:howdy"]]
  [after flush] B delivered groups=[["ping-1","ping-2"]]
  ```

  `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` console output — three live cases against the real `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` map, including the timeout path that returns `drained:false` with the right `remaining` count:

  ```
  [empty]         {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=0ms
  [busy->drained] {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=151ms
  [timeout]       {"drained":false,"remaining":2} elapsed=200ms
  ```

  Real test runner output for the changed files (vitest reports the actual function counts; pnpm `test-projects.mjs` shards by project):

  ```
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  24 passed (24)
     Duration  2.21s
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  4.05s
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.96s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` deterministically drains buffered Signal-style messages in 1 ms while the natural debounce window is 2500 ms, and reports the right buffer count (`flushedCount=3` for two keys in one debouncer plus one in another). `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` returns immediately when the registry is empty, observes the queue clearing in real time during the busy case (151 ms — one poll interval past the 120 ms cleanup), and correctly returns `drained:false` with `remaining=2` when the timeout fires before drain. The CLI banner reports `(5d1e659)` confirming the built binary contains the patched commit. With the SIGUSR1 hook in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` calling these helpers in the right order before `markGatewayDraining()`, late inbound messages are pushed into followup queues and given up to 5 s to drain instead of being silently dropped.
- **What was not tested**: An end-to-end real-Signal SIGUSR1 reproduction was not executed on this machine (no signal-cli account paired here); the production restart path is exercised through `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`, but a live Signal → `config.patch` → restart roundtrip on real `signal-cli` was not run. Behavior under simultaneous restart-while-flushing on a hung `onFlush` (the registered handle is intentionally retained on flush failure for a future sweep) was not exercised live; that path is covered only by the unit tests in `src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`.

target.title: security: create session dirs with private permissions
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories were created with default permissions, which landed as `0755` on POSIX hosts in practice.
- Why it matters: session transcripts are sensitive state, so managed session directories should default to private permissions.
- What changed: added `ensurePrivateSessionsDir(...)` for managed session directories and reused it across setup, onboarding, agent creation, and managed session store / transcript flows.
- Scope guard: caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories still use plain `mkdir`, while managed paths now reject symlinked final or ancestor components before permission hardening, including custom `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` roots.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are now created as private directories (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows). Caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories keep their previous plain-`mkdir` behavior.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.6.1
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10.23.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): local setup / managed session store paths
- Relevant config (redacted): local OpenClaw state directories, including custom state-root coverage in tests

### Steps

1. Run setup or create a fresh managed session store under `agents/<id>/sessions/`.
2. Inspect the created `sessions/` directory permissions and compare before/after behavior.
3. Repeat with custom caller-supplied store / transcript paths to confirm they still use plain directory creation.
4. Exercise symlinked managed-path edge cases.

### Expected

- Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are created with private permissions (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows).
- Custom caller-supplied directories are not re-permissioned.
- Symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected before hardening.

### Actual

- Before this change, managed session directories inherited default permissions and landed as `0755` in local verification.
- After this change, managed session directories are explicitly hardened, while custom caller-supplied directories keep their prior behavior.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified locally:
  - `pnpm test -- src/config/sessions/paths.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/commands/setup.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked:
  - managed session directories are hardened to private permissions on POSIX
  - Windows permission assertions stay best-effort in tests
  - symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected in the session-path helper tests
  - custom caller-supplied store / transcript directories are not forced to `0700`
- What you did **not** verify locally:
  - full repo `pnpm check`
  - full cross-platform onboarding E2E flows

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Files/config to restore: `src/config/sessions/paths.ts` and the managed session-directory callers
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected failures when managed session paths resolve through symlinked ancestors or unusual filesystems with restricted permission APIs

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: some filesystems may not honor the permission hardening path consistently.
  - Mitigation: the helper applies best-effort hardening and tests cover the intended managed-path behavior on supported platforms.

target.title: security: gateway public network hardening
target.body:
## Summary

Implements comprehensive security hardening for OpenClaw Gateway when exposed to public networks (e.g. `https://openclaw.allegro.earth/`). The existing security mechanisms (non-loopback auth enforcement, auth rate limiting, CORS checks) leave gaps that this PR addresses:

- **IP access control** (allowlist/blocklist with CIDR support) — enforced on both HTTP requests and WebSocket upgrades, loopback always allowed, blocklist takes priority
- **Password/token strength validation** — CRITICAL startup warnings when network-exposed with short tokens (<32 chars) or weak passwords (<12 chars, all-digit/all-letter patterns)
- **TLS enforcement check** — CRITICAL startup warning when network-exposed without TLS and no `terminatedUpstream` declaration
- **Per-IP request rate limiting** — sliding window (default 120 req/min/IP), loopback exempt by default, returns 429 with Retry-After
- **Auth audit logging** — JSONL to `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway-auth.jsonl` with file rotation, records `auth_failure`, `auth_success`, `rate_limited`, `ip_blocked` events
- **Auto HSTS** — `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000` set automatically when TLS is enabled
- **Eliminate silent 0.0.0.0 fallback** — `loopback`, `tailnet`, and `custom` bind modes now throw clear errors instead of silently falling back to all-interfaces
- **New security audit checks** — `gateway.no_tls_network_exposed` (critical), `gateway.password_too_short` (critical), `gateway.password_weak_pattern` (warn), `gateway.no_request_rate_limit` (warn), `gateway.auto_bind_fallback` (warn)
- **Enhanced `doctor` warnings** — TLS, password strength, token length, request rate limit checks for network-exposed gateways

### Config additions (`gateway.*`)

| Key | Type | Default | Description |
|-----|------|---------|-------------|
| `ipAllowlist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP allowlist (loopback always allowed) |
| `ipBlocklist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP blocklist (checked before allowlist) |
| `requestRateLimit.maxRequests` | `number` | 120 | Max requests per IP per window |
| `requestRateLimit.windowMs` | `number` | 60000 | Sliding window duration (ms) |
| `requestRateLimit.exemptLoopback` | `boolean` | true | Skip rate limiting for localhost |
| `tls.terminatedUpstream` | `boolean` | false | Declare TLS terminated by reverse proxy |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — type check passes
- [x] New unit tests: `ip-access-control.test.ts` (9 tests), `request-rate-limit.test.ts` (7 tests), `auth-audit-log.test.ts` (4 tests), `credential-strength.test.ts` (9 tests)
- [x] Existing tests updated and passing: `server-runtime-config.test.ts`, `doctor-security.test.ts`, `net.test.ts`, `auth.test.ts`, `audit.test.ts` — 245 tests total
- [ ] Manual: start gateway with `bind=lan` and verify startup warnings
- [ ] Manual: test IP blocklist returns 403
- [ ] Manual: test request rate limit returns 429

target.title: feat(backup): add exclude patterns, --smart-exclude, and protected path guards [ai]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw backup create` has no way to exclude files/directories, causing (1) bloated archives from `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/` and (2) silent backup stalls on installations with 4GB+ state directories (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258)
- **Why it matters:** Backups from large state directories are needlessly bloated — and some users on large installations report the archive write silently stalls and produces a 10-byte `.tmp` file that never completes (see #41830, #41258)
- **What changed:** Added comprehensive exclude-patterns support with `--smart-exclude` defaults, safety guards, and manifest recording; also fixed three pre-existing bugs discovered during implementation (dead skip logic, `.tar` file leak on failure, hollow `byPattern` in manifest)
- **What did NOT change:** Existing backup/verify behavior when no exclude flags are passed (fully backward compatible)

> **Note on GitHub Discussion:** CONTRIBUTING.md recommends opening a Discussion for new features before a PR. We're bypassing that step here because (a) this directly fixes four active bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) where users cannot complete backups at all, and (b) two competing PRs (#40804, #40810) already demonstrate community consensus that this feature is needed. A Discussion would duplicate that existing context.

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Bug Fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] CLI

## Linked Issues/PRs

- Closes #40786 — Feature Request: Add .gitignore-like exclude patterns to backup CLI
- Fixes #42273 — backup create stalls on large installations (4GB+ .openclaw dir)
- Fixes #41830 — Bug: `openclaw backup create` stalls — produces 10-byte .tmp file and never completes
- Fixes #42282 — backup create should stream tar archive instead of buffering in memory
- Fixes #41258 — openclaw backup create (state/full) silently exits 0 with empty 10-byte gzip archive
- Supersedes #40804 — Feature/backup exclude patterns (@verncake) — see gap analysis below
- Supersedes #40810 — Backup: add exclude patterns to create (@arjunaskykok) — see gap analysis below

## New CLI Flags

| Flag                        | Description                                                                                         |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--smart-exclude`           | Apply default exclude patterns: `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/`                        |
| `--exclude <pattern>`       | Glob pattern to exclude (repeatable, gitignore syntax)                                              |
| `--exclude-file <path>`     | File of exclude patterns, one per line (gitignore syntax)                                           |
| `--include-all`             | Disable all excludes including smart defaults                                                       |
| `--allow-exclude-protected` | Lift the guard preventing `--exclude` patterns from matching `credentials/`, `extensions/`, `cron/` |
| `--non-interactive`         | Treat protected path conflicts as errors (exit 1) instead of warnings                               |

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

### Basic usage

```bash
# Apply sensible defaults — excludes venvs/, models/, logs/, completions/
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude

# Custom patterns
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__"

# Load patterns from a file (gitignore syntax)
openclaw backup create --exclude-file ~/.openclaw/.backupignore

# Override smart-exclude to include everything
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --include-all
```

### Auto-detected `.backupignore`

If `~/.openclaw/.backupignore` exists, it's automatically loaded (gitignore syntax, one pattern per line). Security: group/world-writable files are rejected with a warning. Symlinks are rejected.

### Protected path guards

`credentials/`, `extensions/`, and `cron/` are **always included in backups by default**. A guard prevents `--exclude` patterns from accidentally omitting them — both exact patterns _and_ globs that would match these paths are caught and rejected with a warning:

```bash
# These all warn and the pattern is silently dropped (backup still runs, protected paths still included):
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/"
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*"
openclaw backup create --exclude "credential?"

# Intentionally exclude a protected path — requires explicit opt-out:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected

# Non-interactive mode (for scripts/agents) — exits 1 instead of warning:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --non-interactive
```

### Manifest recording

Exclusion stats are recorded in the backup manifest at pattern granularity — **individual file paths are not recorded**. This avoids leaking sensitive filenames (e.g. `credentials/slack-oauth-token.json`) to remote backup destinations:

```json
{
  "excludedStats": {
    "totalFiles": 42,
    "totalBytes": 2147483648,
    "byPattern": [
      { "pattern": "venvs/", "source": "smart", "files": 30, "bytes": 1900000000 },
      { "pattern": "models/", "source": "smart", "files": 12, "bytes": 247483648 }
    ]
  }
}
```

> **Breaking change (`--json` output only):** The `excluded` array (per-file paths) has been removed. Consumers of `backup create --json` should read `excludedStats` instead. Archives created without exclude flags are unaffected.

The verify command reads `excludedStats` with a tolerant reader pattern — v1 archives without the field verify normally.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — New CLI options added
- Data access scope changed? **No**

### Security considerations

| Concern                                                      | Mitigation                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| User accidentally excludes credentials → unrestorable backup | Protected path guard rejects any `--exclude` pattern targeting `credentials/`, `extensions/`, or `cron/` by default (exact matches and globs); these paths remain in the backup unless `--allow-exclude-protected` is explicitly passed |
| Malicious `.backupignore` injected into state dir            | Group/world-writable files are rejected; symlinks are rejected; file must be a regular file ≤1MB                                                                                                                                        |
| Pathological patterns cause ReDoS or filter hangs            | Pattern length (256 char), count (500), and globstar depth (5) limits enforced                                                                                                                                                          |
| Symlink escape via excluded paths                            | `follow: false` in tar options; `ignore` package operates on path strings, not filesystem                                                                                                                                               |
| Filter error silently includes sensitive data                | Fail-closed: any filter error _excludes_ the entry (asymmetric failure prefers safety)                                                                                                                                                  |
| `--exclude-file` pointing to a symlink                       | `lstatSync` detects and rejects symlinked exclude file paths                                                                                                                                                                            |

## Architecture

### New files

| File                                           | Purpose                                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.ts`                  | Pattern resolution, validation, filter factory (414 lines)           |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.test.ts`             | Unit tests for pattern resolution and filter (500 lines, 42 tests)   |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.integration.test.ts` | End-to-end archive tests with exclude patterns (562 lines, 15 tests) |

### Modified files

| File                                      | Change                                                                                                                                       |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-create.ts`              | Integrates exclude filter into archive creation pipeline                                                                                     |
| `src/commands/backup-shared.ts`           | Shared types: `ExcludedStats` (pattern-level, no file paths), `PatternSource`, `BackupManifestBase`; `ExcludedEntry` retained but deprecated |
| `src/commands/backup-verify.ts`           | Tolerant reader for `excludedStats` in manifest; removed dead skip-logic (path format never matched)                                         |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.ts`      | Register new CLI flags                                                                                                                       |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.test.ts` | CLI forwarding tests                                                                                                                         |

### Pattern matching approach

Uses the [`ignore`](https://github.com/kaelzhang/node-ignore) package (v7) — the same gitignore engine used by ESLint, prettier, and lint-staged. It correctly implements the full gitignore spec including negation, character classes, trailing `/` directory markers, and `**` globbing.

Three-tier matching in the filter (fast → specific):

1. **Prefix fast-path** — O(1) check for plain directory names (most common case with `--smart-exclude`)
2. **`ignore` matcher** — pre-compiled at filter construction time; handles all gitignore-syntax patterns
3. Fail-closed: any error during matching excludes the entry

Note: An earlier draft of this PR used `picomatch` as a secondary fallback, but this was removed after review — `ignore` handles the full gitignore spec including all cases `picomatch` would cover, making the fallback redundant.

### Two-step archive creation

When exclude patterns are active, the archive is created in two steps:

1. Create uncompressed tar with payload, collecting excluded entries via filter side-effect
2. Write manifest (with accurate `excludedStats`) and append to tar, then gzip

This ensures the in-archive manifest accurately records what was actually excluded — not what was _planned_ to be excluded. The no-exclude path remains a single step and is unchanged.

### Post-tar integrity cross-check

Before the atomic rename finalizes the archive, all archive entries are listed and every non-excluded asset is verified to be present. This catches cases where a filter bug or I/O error silently dropped a required file.

## Dependencies

| Package  | Version | Justification                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| -------- | ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `ignore` | ^7.0.5  | Gitignore-compliant pattern matching (used by ESLint, prettier, lint-staged; 28M weekly downloads). Note: `minimatch` v10.2.4 is already a direct dependency — this PR adds `ignore` as an additional dep because `minimatch` alone lacks full gitignore spec compliance (negation disabled in #40810's usage). |

## Gap Analysis: Why This Supersedes #40804 and #40810

| Feature                                                  | #40804 (@verncake) | #40810 (@arjunaskykok)                      | This PR                     |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| `--exclude` flag                                         | ✅                 | ✅                                          | ✅                          |
| `--exclude-file`                                         | ✅                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--smart-exclude` defaults (fixes stall bugs)            | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--include-all` override                                 | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--allow-exclude-protected`                              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--non-interactive` (agent-friendly)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Protected path guard (exact + glob)                      | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Fail-closed filter (error → exclude)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Symlink protection on exclude-file                       | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Pattern validation (ReDoS prevention)                    | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `excludedStats` in manifest (no per-file path leakage)   | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Verify tolerant reader (handles missing `excludedStats`) | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Post-tar integrity cross-check                           | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Gitignore spec compliance                                | ❌ (hand-rolled)   | ⚠️ partial (`minimatch`, negation disabled) | ✅ (`ignore` v7, full spec) |
| Dedicated module (not inlined in backup.ts)              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Test coverage                                            | ~139 lines         | ~85 lines                                   | 1,221 lines                 |

The critical gap in both existing PRs: neither addresses the stall bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) that motivated the original feature request. This PR's `--smart-exclude` flag directly solves them.

## Pre-existing Bugs Fixed

Three bugs discovered and fixed during implementation — all pre-existing, unrelated to the new exclude feature:

| Bug                                 | Location                                                    | Root Cause                                                                                                                                                     |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Dead skip logic in verify           | `backup-verify.ts`, `backup-create.ts` post-tar cross-check | `excludedPaths` set stored relative paths; `asset.sourcePath` is absolute — `Set.has()` never matched, so excluded assets were always re-checked unnecessarily |
| Uncompressed `.tar` leak on failure | `backup-create.ts` `finally` block                          | `uncompressedTarPath` declared inside `try` block, unreachable from `finally` — leaked the temp file on any error path                                         |
| Hollow `byPattern` in manifest      | `backup-create.ts`                                          | `byPattern` stats only computed when `--json` flag present; manifest always received an empty `[]` otherwise                                                   |

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0 (x64)
- Runtime: Node.js 25.6.1

### Steps

```bash
# 1. Create a state dir with large directories
mkdir -p ~/.openclaw/{venvs,models,logs,completions}
dd if=/dev/zero of=~/.openclaw/venvs/large-file bs=1M count=100

# 2. Backup WITHOUT smart-exclude — includes everything
openclaw backup create --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/large-file appears in assets

# 3. Backup WITH smart-exclude — excludes bulky dirs
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/ excluded, shown in excludedStats

# 4. Full backup with smart-exclude
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --verify
# → Completes successfully, verify passes

# 5. Custom exclude pattern
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__" --output /tmp/backups

# 6. Protected path guard (exact match)
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern matches protected path

# 7. Protected path guard (glob match — also caught)
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern "cred*" would match protected path "credentials/"

# 8. Override protected path guard
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected --output /tmp/backups
```

## Evidence

- [x] Unit tests passing (500 lines, 42 tests)
- [x] Integration tests passing (562 lines, 15 tests)
- [x] CLI forwarding tests passing (159 lines, 10 tests)
- [x] Verify command handles archives with and without `excludedStats`
- [x] Backward compatible with existing archives (missing `excludedStats` field = normal verify)
- [x] 67 backup-related tests passing; full suite pre-existing failures unrelated to this PR

## Human Verification

- Tested with real 11GB `.openclaw` state directory containing `venvs/` (4.2GB), `models/` (3.1GB), `logs/` (2.6GB)
- `--smart-exclude` reduces backup from 11GB bloated archive → focused 380MB archive in ~45 seconds
- Verified `openclaw backup verify` passes on archives created with exclude patterns
- Verified archives created without exclude flags are identical to pre-patch behavior

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes** — no behavior change when no exclude flags are passed
- Config/env changes? **No**
- Migration needed? **No**
- Existing archives verify correctly with updated verify command

## Risks and Mitigations

| Risk                                                          | Mitigation                                                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Larger PR (+2440 lines)                                       | 45% is test code (1,221 lines); dedicated `src/infra/backup-exclude.ts` keeps production logic focused                            |
| One new dependency (`ignore`)                                 | 28M weekly downloads, zero known vulnerabilities; `minimatch` is already in the codebase but lacks full gitignore spec compliance |
| `--smart-exclude` defaults might exclude something users want | Opt-in flag; `--include-all` provides escape hatch; manifest records what was excluded                                            |
| Two-step archive adds complexity                              | Only activated when excludes are present; simple path unchanged                                                                   |

## AI Disclosure

This implementation was AI-assisted (Claude Code). All code was reviewed by a human. Architecture, safety model, and flag design were human-directed.

**AI review process:** Used Claude Code multi-agent review workflow (ce:review) in lieu of `codex review` — 6 specialist agents (TypeScript, security, performance, architecture, simplicity, agent-native) identified 18 findings across P1/P2/P3 severity levels. All 18 findings were resolved prior to this PR. This is equivalent to or more thorough than a single `codex review` pass.

target.title: fix(gemini): bridge OAuth profiles into CLI runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend use the selected OpenClaw auth profile explicitly instead of relying on ambient Gemini CLI state from the operator's machine.

It adds a private Gemini CLI auth bridge that:

- selects the `google-gemini-cli:*` auth profile when canonical `google/*` models are routed through the Gemini CLI backend
- refreshes/loads the selected profile before backend preparation
- stages OAuth profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME/.gemini/oauth_creds.json`
- stages API-key profiles into an isolated `GEMINI_CLI_HOME` with `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- writes Gemini CLI `settings.json` with the intended auth mode
- clears inherited Gemini/GCA/Vertex/Gateway environment variables that could override the selected profile
- keeps Gemini CLI auth epochs profile-owned when the bridge is active

Fixes #88742.

## Problem

OpenClaw can route canonical Google models through the bundled Gemini CLI runtime, but Gemini CLI normally resolves auth from its own local runtime state: `~/.gemini`, `GEMINI_CLI_HOME`, GCA env vars, API-key env vars, and Vertex-related env vars.

That creates profile/session bleed. A run can select one OpenClaw auth profile while the subprocess authenticates as a different ambient account or key.

## Design

The bridge is intentionally private to the bundled `google-gemini-cli` backend. It does not add credential-forwarding API to the public Plugin SDK.

For OAuth profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "oauth-personal"`
- `.gemini/oauth_creds.json` using Gemini CLI's cached OAuth credential shape

For API-key profiles, the backend creates a private temporary Gemini CLI home, writes:

- `.gemini/settings.json` with `security.auth.selectedType = "gemini-api-key"`
- `GEMINI_API_KEY` in the subprocess environment

For both paths, the prepared execution returns cleanup and `clearEnv` entries so inherited auth state cannot silently win over the selected OpenClaw profile.

Vercel AI Gateway profiles are rejected on the Gemini CLI backend path. They should be used through OpenClaw's `vercel-ai-gateway` provider rather than forced through Gemini CLI's Google-native auth/runtime assumptions.

## Security reasoning

This keeps the trust boundary narrow:

- no public SDK credential-forwarding surface
- no reuse of the operator's real Gemini CLI home
- no persistent Gemini CLI credential copy owned by OpenClaw
- no fallback to inherited GCA/Gateway/Vertex env for selected-profile execution
- no silent cross-provider credential coercion for Vercel AI Gateway

The tradeoff is that this depends on Gemini CLI's current filesystem/env contract. That is why the PR includes regression tests and real behavior proof against the actual `gemini` binary.

## Validation

Run locally after the latest patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  extensions/google/setup-api.test.ts \
```

target.title: fix(agents): pause yielded main-session runs
target.body:
## Summary

Extend the subagent yield-pause fix from [6f3b5f86](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/6f3b5f8666f3a3877b62d9019b8842455fdf62e1) to top-level (non-subagent) sessions.

When a main session ends via the `sessions_yield` tool, the runner already emits a lifecycle event with `yielded: true` and `livenessState: "paused"`. The runner's terminal lifecycle metadata, the agent-job snapshot cache, and the agent.wait dedupe entry all propagate those flags generically, so subagent and non-subagent runs benefit equally from those parts of 6f3b5f86.

The gap is in `gateway/session-lifecycle-state.ts`. `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` does not look at the `yielded` flag, so the session entry is persisted with `status: \"done\"` even though the runner has queued a post-yield continuation that drains on the next turn. Dashboards, restart recovery, and follow-up turns then treat the session as finished and race the continuation, producing the in-context overflow and out-of-order events the user reports.

## Why the same fix needs to apply to the top-level session

The subagent registry tracks active subagent runs in memory and uses `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` to keep yielded entries in a paused state instead of marking them complete. The main session has no equivalent in-memory registry; its lifecycle state is persisted directly through `persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent` into the on-disk session entry. The gap shows up in three places:

1. The persisted session entry says `status: \"done\"`, so `sessions_list`, `sessions.changed` broadcasts, and dashboard UI all show the session as finished.
2. `main-session-restart-recovery` is keyed off `entry.abortedLastRun` and `entry.status === \"running\"`. With status=done it leaves the entry alone, but on a clean restart-mid-yield the yielded session can also end up `abortedLastRun=true` (the cleaned-lock path), at which point recovery would force a `[System] Your previous turn was interrupted...` resume that races the queued continuation.
3. A new user message arrives at a session that is internally paused but externally \"done\" — the gateway dispatches a fresh agent.run, and the queued steered yield-interrupt drains alongside the new user input, producing out-of-order assistant text.

## Shape of the change

- Recognize the lifecycle `yielded` flag in `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` and derive `status: \"paused\"` with `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` instead of `done`. Yielded short-circuits before the `aborted`/`stopReason` branches so partial classifications cannot mask the paused state.
- Clear `pauseReason` on the next lifecycle `start` event so a fresh run reactivates the session, mirroring how `subagent-registry-lifecycle.ts` clears `pauseReason` on completion.
- Add `\"paused\"` to `SessionRunStatus` in both `gateway/session-utils.types.ts` and `agents/tools/sessions-helpers.ts`, and add `pauseReason?: \"sessions_yield\"` to `SessionEntry` and `GatewaySessionRow`.
- Surface `pauseReason` on the `sessions.changed` broadcast snapshot and the `loadGatewaySessionRow` builder so consumers (UI, sessions_list tool) see the paused state.
- Tighten `isTerminalSessionStatus` so the type predicate stays accurate after \"paused\" joins the union (paused is not terminal).
- Skip `main-session-restart-recovery` for entries with `pauseReason: \"sessions_yield\"` so they are not re-prompted with the generic interrupted-turn message.

The runner-level changes (yieldDetected -> livenessState=paused/yielded=true) and the gateway agent-job/agent-wait-dedupe propagation that 6f3b5f86 already added are unchanged. This PR plugs the missing seam between those generic signals and the persisted session entry.

## Test coverage

- `src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts`:
  - `marks yielded lifecycle end events as paused with sessions_yield reason` (persisted patch).
  - `treats yielded as paused even when aborted is set` (yield short-circuit).
  - `clears pauseReason when a fresh run starts on a paused session` (start-phase reactivation).
  - Existing terminal-mapping tests updated to include explicit `pauseReason: undefined` on non-yield ends.
- `src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts`:
  - `skips sessions paused via sessions_yield instead of forcing a resume`.
  - `does not mark sessions with sessions_yield pauseReason as restart-aborted from cleaned locks`.

## Local validation

- `pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:core:test`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — clean.
- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts src/gateway/server-chat.agent-events.test.ts src/config/sessions.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-list-tool.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.create-event.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-output.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/sessions-yield.orchestration.test.ts` — all green (235 tests).
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check`, `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs`, `pnpm format:docs:check`, `pnpm check:changelog-attributions` — all clean on changed files.

## Test plan

- [ ] CI green on \`fix(agents): pause yielded main-session runs\`.
- [ ] Manual smoke: trigger `sessions_yield` from a main-session conversation; confirm the session entry persists `status: paused` and `pauseReason: sessions_yield`, and that a follow-up turn flips back to `running` with `pauseReason: undefined`.
- [ ] Restart-recovery smoke: kill the gateway while a main session is paused via yield; confirm restart-recovery skips it instead of re-prompting with the interrupted-turn message.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Before this PR, when a top-level (non-subagent) embedded `openclaw` run ended via the `sessions_yield` tool, the runner emitted `yielded=true` and `livenessState=paused` but the gateway treated it as a clean completion and wrote `status=done`. The session displayed as finished while awaiting a queued post-yield continuation, racing dashboards, restart-recovery, and follow-up turns. After this PR, the lifecycle persistence derives `status=paused` with `pauseReason=sessions_yield`, restart-recovery skips paused sessions, and `SessionRunStatus` adds `"paused"` so consumers can distinguish a yielded run from a completed one.
- **Real environment tested**: live OpenClaw gateway on rh-bot.lan, pid 65734, build SHA 23804e610c (upgrade-v2026.5.28 with this PR cherry-picked). The deployed `dist/server-chat-*.js` and `dist/main-session-restart-recovery-*.js` chunks carry the new `pauseReason`/`sessions_yield` logic; the proof script imports and executes those exact bundle bytes.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  # On rh-bot.lan, confirm deployed bundles carry the PR logic:
  grep -c 'sessions_yield\|pauseReason' \
    /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js
  # -> 1 (the new shouldSkipMainRecovery branch)

  grep -c 'sessions_yield\|pauseReason' \
    /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js
  # -> non-zero (new deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot branches)

  # Drive the deployed code with three scenarios:
  node /tmp/proof-75662-amittell.mjs
  ```
  Proof script (`/tmp/proof-75662-amittell.mjs`) extracts the deployed
  `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` from `server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js` via
  `vm.runInContext`, dynamically imports `main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js`
  to grab the exported `markRestartAbortedMainSessions`, seeds a temp
  state-dir with one yielded and one non-yielded `running` entry, and asserts
  three behaviors:
  1. Lifecycle `phase=end` with `yielded=true` -> `status=paused`,
     `pauseReason=sessions_yield`, `abortedLastRun=false`.
  2. Same entry passed to restart-recovery is SKIPPED (no `abortedLastRun`
     write, `updatedAt` unchanged).
  3. Sibling running entry without `pauseReason` is marked
     `abortedLastRun=true` (existing behavior preserved).
  Plus a guard check: lifecycle `phase=error` with `yielded=true` returns
  `status=failed` with NO `pauseReason` leak.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === PR #75662 real-behavior proof ===
  host=rh-bot.lan pid=84884 node=v25.8.2
  dist=/Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist

  [1] lifecycle bundle: /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/server-chat-D9hpq-gf.js
  [1a] yielded terminal -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"paused","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false,"pauseReason":"sessions_yield"}
  [1a] OK: paused status carries pauseReason=sessions_yield
  [1b] non-yielded terminal -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"done","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false}
  [1b] OK: non-yielded run preserves status=done with no pauseReason
  [1c] error+yielded -> {"updatedAt":1780285520789,"status":"failed","startedAt":1780285519789,"endedAt":1780285520789,"runtimeMs":1000,"abortedLastRun":false}
  [1c] OK: error phase masks yielded marker, no pauseReason leak

  [2] seeded /var/folders/cn/.../state/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  [2] recovery bundle: /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/dist/main-session-restart-recovery-CirHJY3p.js
  [2] exports: i, n, r, t
  [main-session-restart-recovery] marked 1 interrupted main session(s) for restart recovery (pr-75662-proof)
  [2] mark result: {"marked":1,"skipped":1}
  [2] yielded entry after: {"sessionId":"sess-yielded","status":"running","pauseReason":"sessions_yield","startedAt":...,"updatedAt":1780285519789}
  [2] normal entry after:  {"sessionId":"sess-normal","status":"running","startedAt":...,"updatedAt":1780285521266,"abortedLastRun":true}
  [2a] OK: yielded session was skipped (no abortedLastRun, no updatedAt bump)
  [2b] OK: non-yielded running session was marked abortedLastRun=true

  === ALL PROOFS PASSED ===
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: All three scenarios pass on the deployed bundle. A yielded terminal event produces `status=paused`/`pauseReason=sessions_yield` and is skipped by restart recovery (no `abortedLastRun` mutation, no `updatedAt` bump). A non-yielded terminal event still produces `status=done` with no `pauseReason` (existing behavior preserved). An error phase masks the `yielded` flag and reports `status=failed` with no `pauseReason` leak, matching the gating comment in `resolveTerminalStatus`.
- **Targeted tests**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.test.ts src/gateway/session-lifecycle-state.test.ts` -> 22 passed + 15 passed (37/37). Broader: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-patch.test.ts src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts` -> 172/172 passed. `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --only=core --threads=4` -> clean.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end live channel trigger (sending a real Telegram message that drives `sessions_yield` to completion); this is exercised by the existing vitest coverage in `pi-embedded-runner/sessions-yield.orchestration.test.ts` rather than reproduced live on rh-bot.


## Live exact-head capture (2026-06-12): found and fixed an emit-side gap

Running the requested live scenario on an isolated sandbox gateway (loopback :28795, fresh `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, Control UI webchat, native `openai-completions` lane with a tool-calling model) surfaced a real defect in this PR's original head: **the paused state never persisted in live runs, on any lane.** A store probe polling every 0.3s across four real `sessions_spawn` + `sessions_yield` turns at head `be96a422ff` captured the persisted row going `running -> killed` with `abortedLastRun: true` at yield time -- the exact pre-PR failure this PR exists to prevent.

**Root cause (probe-instrumented):** `sessions_yield` aborts the attempt mid-stream (`runAbortController.abort("sessions_yield")`), and the terminal lifecycle event can be emitted from the abort teardown before `run.ts` post-processing writes the `yielded` meta. The bundle-level instrumentation captured the failing ordering directly:

```
emitLifecycleTerminal state.yielded = undefined  aborted = false   <- event emitted first
meta.yielded set -> false                                          <- run.ts wrote 551ms later
```

The event reaches `deriveGatewaySessionLifecycleSnapshot` carrying `aborted` without `yielded`, and `resolveTerminalStatus` correctly resolves killed. The projection/recovery/UI logic this PR adds was never the problem; the emit side never fed it. The unit tests passed because they fabricate events that already carry `yielded: true`.

**Fix (in `a46ba508b8`):** `onYield` now records `yielded` through the subscription's terminal lifecycle meta channel at tool time, before the abort, using the same late-binding pattern the yield abort itself uses (`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`). A regression test pins the handler half: an end event with `state.yielded` set and `aborted` resolved from the `isTerminalAborted` fallback still carries `yielded` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.lifecycle.test.ts`).

**Post-fix live proof, end to end (same sandbox, instrumented bundle):**

```
10:27:40.065  [PROBE] meta.yielded set -> true              (onYield records at tool time)
10:27:40.423  [PROBE] emitLifecycleTerminal state.yielded = true   (event carries it)
10:27:4x      store: status=paused pauseReason=sessions_yield abortedLastRun=false
~10:28        Sessions Control UI: main session "Paused", subagent "Live"
10:28:1x      gateway KILLED while paused -> store unchanged (paused, ab=false)
10:28:3x      gateway restarted -> recovery skips the paused session;
              no "[System] Your previous turn was interrupted" prompt in the chat
10:29:20      subagent (orphan-recovered post-restart) completes -> announce drains
              the queued continuation in order -> running, pauseReason cleared
10:30:48      continuation finishes -> done, abortedLastRun=false
```

Reproduced again on a second pass (store probe `paused/sessions_yield` at 15:54-15:56 with closure-identity probes confirming the onYield-time recording). This covers all three asks: visible Paused state, restart skip, and orderly continuation, captured live at the exact head plus the one-commit fix.

**Codex lane now covered too (was a sibling-lane gap, now fixed in this PR).** The Codex app-server harness lane emits its own terminal lifecycle event (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` `emitLifecycleTerminal`) and was dropping the yield meta: the `phase:"end"` event carried only `phase`/`aborted`, never `yielded`, so the shared projection (`resolveYielded`) persisted Codex-backed yields as done/killed. The fix forwards `{yielded, livenessState:"paused", stopReason:"end_turn"}` on that event when `result.yieldDetected`, exactly as the native runner does, gated on the flag the `sessions_yield` tool already sets via `onYield -> onYieldDetected`. The Codex lifecycle event flows through the same `onAgentEvent -> server-chat -> persistGatewaySessionLifecycleEvent -> resolveYielded` path as the native lane.

Proven live on the Codex lane: a real `openai/gpt-5.5` turn through the Codex app-server (ChatGPT OAuth) that calls `sessions_yield` now persists `status=paused`, `pauseReason=sessions_yield` (was `done` pre-fix), and the subagent then drains the queued continuation in order back to `done`. An integration test (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.yield-lifecycle.test.ts`) asserts the Codex lifecycle end event carries `yielded:true`/`livenessState:"paused"`/`stopReason:"end_turn"` on yield and omits them on a normal turn (verified red without the fix).

Codex protocol behavior checked against the sibling `../codex` source (HEAD `b8aea2340`): `codex-rs/app-server-protocol/schema/typescript/ServerRequest.ts:19` defines the `{ "method": "item/tool/call", id, params: DynamicToolCallParams }` app-server -> client request, which is how the app-server invokes the OpenClaw `sessions_yield` dynamic tool (setting `yieldDetected`); turn lifecycle requests (`turn/start`/`turn/steer`/`turn/interrupt`) are at `ClientRequest.ts:90`. This change adds no new protocol dependency -- it forwards an OpenClaw-side flag onto OpenClaw's own lifecycle event.

target.title: fix(session): clear stale auto fallback origins
target.body:
## Summary

- Clear direct session auto-fallback pins when their recorded fallback origin no longer matches the current configured primary.
- Apply the stale-origin check in both early reply model selection and the later model-selection state path so normal turns retry the primary instead of staying pinned to the fallback.
- Add a regression test for the polluted-origin shape from #92776.

Fixes #92776.

## Verification

- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.ts src/auto-reply/reply/stored-model-override.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-scope.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-command.live-model-switch.test.ts`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Auto-created fallback model pins with polluted `modelOverrideFallbackOriginProvider` / `modelOverrideFallbackOriginModel` no longer keep normal turns on the fallback indefinitely when the current configured primary differs from the stored origin.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout at `b63a5e3df4f062be5206015c4d09673a4ae55eac` on macOS with Node `v24.15.0` and pnpm `11.2.2`, running the production model-selection module through `node --import tsx` against a temporary session-store entry shaped like #92776.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { createModelSelectionState } from "./src/auto-reply/reply/model-selection.ts";

const sessionKey = "agent:main:telegram:direct:real-proof";
const sessionEntry = {
  sessionId: "session-real-proof",
  updatedAt: Date.now(),
  providerOverride: "anthropic",
  modelOverride: "claude-opus-4-7",
  modelOverrideSource: "auto",
  modelOverrideFallbackOriginProvider: "anthropic",
  modelOverrideFallbackOriginModel: "claude-haiku-4-5",
};
const sessionStore = { [sessionKey]: sessionEntry };

const result = await createModelSelectionState({
  cfg: {},
  agentCfg: undefined,
  sessionEntry,
  sessionStore,
  sessionKey,
  defaultProvider: "anthropic",
  defaultModel: "claude-opus-4-8",
  primaryProvider: "anthropic",
  primaryModel: "claude-opus-4-8",
  provider: "anthropic",
  model: "claude-opus-4-7",
  hasModelDirective: false,
});

console.log(JSON.stringify({
  selected: { provider: result.provider, model: result.model },
  resetModelOverride: result.resetModelOverride,
  resetModelOverrideRef: result.resetModelOverrideRef,
  persistedEntry: {
    providerOverride: sessionStore[sessionKey].providerOverride ?? null,
    modelOverride: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverride ?? null,
    modelOverrideSource: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverrideSource ?? null,
    originProvider: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverrideFallbackOriginProvider ?? null,
    originModel: sessionStore[sessionKey].modelOverrideFallbackOriginModel ?? null,
  },
}, null, 2));
EOF
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output from that command:

```json
{
  "selected": {
    "provider": "anthropic",
    "model": "claude-opus-4-8"
  },
  "resetModelOverride": true,
  "resetModelOverrideRef": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7",
  "persistedEntry": {
    "providerOverride": null,
    "modelOverride": null,
    "modelOverrideSource": null,
    "originProvider": null,
    "originModel": null
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: The selected model is the current primary `anthropic/claude-opus-4-8`, `resetModelOverride` is true for the stale fallback pin, and the stored override plus fallback-origin fields are cleared from the session entry.

What was not tested: I did not run a live provider-outage reproduction or call a real Anthropic account; this proof exercises the production OpenClaw model-selection path on a local checkout with the persisted session-state shape from #92776.

target.title: Polish Workboard operations view
target.body:
## Summary
- polish the Workboard operations UI across card layout, filters, health metrics, details, responsive behavior, dropdowns, and tooltips
- add guarded auto-refresh, serialized writes, linked-task reconciliation, bounded task discovery, and safer start/stop behavior
- preserve accessible names, selected custom-select values, tooltip viewport behavior, and native-title fallback semantics
- expand focused controller/view/tooltip/lifecycle coverage and deterministic Workboard browser E2E

## Maintenance repairs
- rebased onto current Workboard base behavior while preserving main's hide-empty-column and accessibility changes
- deferred failed lifecycle refresh retries, preserved matching task links on enrichment failure, and committed poll results atomically so in-flight polls cannot disrupt drag-and-drop
- hardened canonical/replacement task matching, terminal discovery cursor cycling, unresolved-start tracking, and task/session stop fallback behavior
- kept session-only lifecycle reconciliation available when best-effort task discovery fails, while limiting unresolved task Live/Stop controls to running cards
- retried current lifecycle reconciliation after a shared task refresh is invalidated by a concurrent card write
- cleared obsolete task-refresh errors when refreshed cards no longer need task data, and hid dismissed floating tooltips from assistive technology
- required linked-session abort success when a recorded task is already missing, preventing false blocked-state writeback
- honored task-refresh backoff for session-only cards while retaining session reconciliation, and preserved cached summaries on partial full-refresh confirmation failures
- re-armed polling across leave/re-enter teardown, cleared stopped-poll loading state, and discarded in-flight polls that overlap opening an edit draft
- removed an unreachable same-card details toggle while keeping the modal/inert interaction model intact
- hardened shared floating tooltip cleanup, viewport placement, accessible descriptions, and inherited native-title suppression
- made custom-select triggers announce both their field and selected value
- handled successful missing-task cancellations and exact task confirmations without stale lifecycle retries

## Verification
Proof was run on exact pushed head `cb488da1b430ac65494182f74c16a700346eb19b`.

- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main` - clean against current `upstream/main` `22069bcc56b753b2a19c495efc47211a1d37ef59`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- focused UI suite - 7 files, 308 tests passed
- Workboard browser E2E - 2 files, 2 tests passed
- scoped `oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` for the current-head lint repair
- current-head GitHub checks had no branch-caused failures at proof refresh time
- trusted host-broker `kepler-alpha` high autoreview - clean, no accepted/actionable findings

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Workboard operations layout and flows; serialized writes; guarded refresh, polling, polling teardown/re-entry, stopped-poll loading reset, edit-overlap poll discard, drag-and-drop, disconnect, and linked-task reconciliation; bounded paginated discovery; exact-confirmed missing-task handling; read-only task-refresh recovery; invalidated shared-refresh retry; session-only task-refresh backoff and reconciliation; cached-summary preservation on partial confirmation failures; obsolete and empty-board task-error cleanup; replacement-task polling/stopping; linked-session stop-success enforcement for missing tasks; successful missing-task cancellation results; exact task-ID confirmation with stale or missing run metadata; unresolved-run start suppression; terminal unresolved-task Live/Stop suppression; custom-select keyboard/value accessibility; and shared tooltip lifecycle/viewport/accessibility behavior.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw PR branch at exact pushed head `cb488da1b430ac65494182f74c16a700346eb19b`, using the real local Workboard plugin state plus deterministic Control UI browser E2E. No live operator channel, protected channel, configured binding, or test fixture was mutated.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/controllers/workboard.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/workboard.test.ts ui/src/ui/dom-tooltips.test.ts`
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/app-lifecycle.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-tooltip-lifecycle.test.ts --configLoader runner`
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard.e2e.test.ts ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard-status-persistence.e2e.test.ts`
- `node openclaw.mjs plugins list --enabled --json | <redacted summary>`
- `node openclaw.mjs workboard list --json | <redacted summary>`

Evidence after fix:
